sections.header: Verses 1-7 — Destruction of the High Places

heb.paragraph: The high places (bamot) were hilltop shrines where Israel practiced syncretistic worship — mixing YHWH worship with Canaanite fertility rites. Despite repeated reform attempts (Hezekiah, Josiah), the bamot always returned. Ezekiel addresses the mountains themselves as if they were guilty co-conspirators in Israel's idolatry.

Ezekiel's favorite pejorative for idols (39 of the 48 OT occurrences are in Ezekiel). The word is related to gelel ('dung pellets') — a deliberately crude, mocking term that reduces pagan deities to excrement. It strips idols of any dignity or power.

refs.note: Josiah's reform destroyed the high places, but they returned after his death. Ezekiel declares that only divine judgment will permanently end syncretistic worship.
Hosea also condemned worship on hilltops and under trees — the high place tradition ran deep in Israel's apostasy.
Paul echoes the prophetic indictment: knowing God, they exchanged his glory for images.

notes.note: Setting the face against the mountains is a hostile prophetic gesture — the prophet becomes God's weapon pointed at the landscape of sin.
Altars broken, incense stands smashed, corpses scattered before the idols — the worshippers will die in the very place where they sinned. Poetic justice at its most literal.
The recognition formula: 'Then you will know that I am the LORD.' This phrase appears over 60 times in Ezekiel — divine judgment has a revelatory purpose.
Calvin notes that God addresses the mountains because the people are too hardened to hear. When the audience refuses to listen, God preaches to creation itself as a witness against human rebellion.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The Hebrew herev ('sword') against the high places is God's weapon, not Babylon's — the foreign army is merely the instrument of divine judgment.
The phrase 'your works will be wiped out' (nimhu) uses language of erasure — God will not merely damage but obliterate every trace of idolatrous worship.

block.source: Daniel I. Block, Ezekiel, NICOT (2 vols., 1997/1998) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Block emphasises the addressee: Ezekiel preaches to the mountains, not the people. This is a declaration of holy war against the land itself. The land that was given as covenant gift has been polluted and must be cleansed by violence.

zimmerli.source: Walther Zimmerli, Ezekiel, Hermeneia (2 vols., 1979/1983) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Zimmerli classifies this as a 'proof-saying' (Erweiswort) — the genre centers on the recognition formula 'then you will know that I am the LORD.' The entire oracle exists to produce knowledge of YHWH through the act of judgment.

hist.context: Ezekiel turns from Jerusalem to address the land itself. The mountains personify the nation — 'mountains of Israel' is a recurring phrase (16 times). By addressing the landscape, Ezekiel indicts the entire territory as polluted by idolatry. The corpses of idolaters will be scattered around their altars, making the high places themselves into graveyards.

sections.header: Verses 8-14 — Scattered Survivors and Total Desolation

heb.paragraph: The recognition formula climaxes here: the survivors scattered among the nations will 'know' (yada) that YHWH is LORD through their experience of judgment. This is not intellectual knowledge but experiential, existential knowledge born of catastrophe. The exiles will understand who God is because they have experienced what he does.

refs.note: The covenant curses predicted scattering among nations and wasting away in guilt — Ezekiel applies this Levitical threat directly.
After scattering, if they return to God with all their heart, he will restore them — the future hope behind the present judgment.

notes.note: The remnant will remember God and loathe themselves for their evil. True repentance involves not just turning to God but genuine revulsion at one's own sin.
Ezekiel is commanded to clap his hands and stamp his feet — bodily gestures of grief and horror at Israel's abominations.
Under every spreading tree and leafy oak — the sites of Canaanite worship. The specific locations reveal how deeply embedded idolatry had become.
From the desert to Diblah — from south to north, the entire land will be desolate. Comprehensive geographical judgment.
Calvin sees the remnant's self-loathing as a mark of genuine repentance. They do not merely regret consequences but grieve the sin itself — the broken adulterous heart that departed from God.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The phrase 'their adulterous hearts' (libbam hazzoneh) uses the imagery of sexual infidelity for idolatry — a metaphor developed extensively in chapters 16 and 23.
Diblah is likely a scribal error for Riblah (a city in northern Syria), creating a merism from the southern desert to the northern border.

block.source: Daniel I. Block, Ezekiel, NICOT (2 vols., 1997/1998) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Block reads the remnant passage as deliberately anti-triumphal: the survivors are not heroes but traumatized witnesses. Their purpose is not to rebuild but to testify — to acknowledge that YHWH is the LORD through their own devastation. The remnant exists for the recognition formula.

zimmerli.source: Walther Zimmerli, Ezekiel, Hermeneia (2 vols., 1979/1983) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Zimmerli identifies the clapping and stamping as ritual gestures of mourning that the prophet performs on God's behalf — God himself grieves the necessity of judgment. The desolation from desert to Riblah encompasses the entire promised land.

hist.context: A surprising pastoral note emerges: God will spare some who escape the sword among the nations (v.8). These survivors will remember God — not with joy but with self-loathing for their sins. The remnant theology here is not triumphant but traumatic: survival means carrying the memory of betrayal. The remnant is saved not for comfort but for witness.

lit.note: An address to the mountains (the land as guilty party), moving from judgment of sacred sites to the traumatic survival of a remnant.

themes.note: Judgment at 9: the high places face total destruction. Worship at 8: the chapter is fundamentally about false worship and its consequences. Mercy at 3: the remnant provides a sliver of hope.

hebtext.note: The high places (bamot) were hilltop shrines where Israel practiced syncretistic worship — mixing YHWH worship with Canaanite fertility rites. Despite repeated reform attempts (Hezekiah, Josiah), the bamot always returned. Ezekiel addresses the mountains themselves as if they were guilty co-conspirators in Israel's idolatry.

Ezekiel's favorite pejorative for idols (39 of the 48 OT occurrences are in Ezekiel). The word is related to gelel ('dung pellets') — a deliberately crude, mocking term that reduces pagan deities to excrement. It strips idols of any dignity or power.

The recognition formula climaxes here: the survivors scattered among the nations will 'know' (yada) that YHWH is LORD through their experience of judgment. This is not intellectual knowledge but experiential, existential knowledge born of catastrophe. The exiles will understand who God is because they have experienced what he does.

ppl.name: Ezekiel
ppl.text: Ezekiel turns from Jerusalem to address the land itself…

trans.title: Comparing translations in Against the Mountains of Israel

translations.text: “ ‘But I will spare some, for some of you will escape the sword when you are scattered among the lands and nations.
“Yet I will leave some of you alive. When you have among the nations some who escape the sword, and when you are scattered through the countries,

Wherever you live, the towns will be laid waste and the high places demolished, so that your altars will be laid waste and devastated, your idols smashed and ruined, your incense altars broken down, and what you have made wiped out.

Wherever you dwell, the cities shall be waste and the high places ruined, so that your altars will be waste and ruined, your idols broken and destroyed, your incense altars cut down, and your works wiped out.

rec.title: John MacArthur
rec.quote: "Setting the face against the mountains is a hostile prophetic gesture — the prophet becomes God's weapon pointed at the landscape of sin."

rec.title: John Calvin
rec.quote: "Calvin notes that God addresses the mountains because the people are too hardened to hear. When the audience refuses to listen, God preaches to creation itself as a witness against human rebellion."

rec.title: NET Bible translators
rec.quote: "The Hebrew herev ('sword') against the high places is God's weapon, not Babylon's — the foreign army is merely the instrument of divine judgment."

thread.text: Josiah's reform destroyed the high places, but they returned after his death. Ezekiel declares that only divine judgment will permanently end syncretistic worship.
Hosea also condemned worship on hilltops and under trees — the high place tradition ran deep in Israel's apostasy.
Paul echoes the prophetic indictment: knowing God, they exchanged his glory for images.
The covenant curses predicted scattering among nations and wasting away in guilt — Ezekiel applies this Levitical threat directly.
After scattering, if they return to God with all their heart, he will restore them — the future hope behind the present judgment.

coaching.tip: Ezekiel prophesies against 'the mountains of Israel' (v. 2) — the high places where idolatry occurred. The landscape itself is addressed because it participated in the sin. Altars, incense stands, and corpses will litter the hills. Ezekiel sees geography as morally implicated: places absorb the character of what happens in them. The land needs cleansing because it hosted corruption.

'Then they will know that I am the LORD' (v. 14) — this recognition formula appears over 70 times in Ezekiel, the book's signature refrain. Every act of judgment and every act of restoration serves the same purpose: God making himself known. The phrase reframes catastrophe: it's not random cruelty but forced revelation. The God who cannot be ignored arranges circumstances that prevent ignoring.
